paraphrase: Barbrey Dustin, Lady of Barrowton, deeply distrusts the maesters, calling them 'grey rats.'

spoken_quote: the words for their own ends
paraphrase: Dustin points out maesters often read and write letters for their lords, so they could possibly twist the words for their own ends.
verbatim: As Maester Medrick went to one knee to whisper in Bolton’s ear, Lady Dustin’s mouth twisted in distaste. “If I were queen, the first thing I would do would be to kill all those grey rats. They scurry everywhere, living on the leavings of the lords, chittering to one another, whispering in the ears of their masters. But who are the masters and who are the servants, truly? Every great lord has his maester, every lesser lord aspires to one. If you do not have a maester, it is taken to mean that you are of little consequence. The grey rats read and write our letters, even for such lords as cannot read themselves, and who can say for a certainty that they are not twisting the words for their own ends? What good are they, I ask you?”

paraphrase: Because maesters give up their family names, Dustin says you can't know who they truly are or where they come from, or whose interests they serve.
verbatim: “That was how it was with Lord Rickard Stark. Maester Walys was his grey rat’s name. And isn’t it clever how the maesters go by only one name, even those who had two when they first arrived at the Citadel? That way we cannot know who they truly are or where they come from … but if you are dogged enough, you can still find out. Before he forged his chain, Maester Walys had been known as Walys Flowers. Flowers, Hill, Rivers, Snow … we give such names to baseborn children to mark them for what they are, but they are always quick to shed them. Walys Flowers had a Hightower girl for a mother … and an archmaester of the Citadel for a father, it was rumored. The grey rats are not as chaste as they would have us believe. Oldtown maesters are the worst of all. Once he forged his chain, his secret father and his friends wasted no time dispatching him to Winterfell to fill Lord Rickard’s ears with poisoned words as sweet as honey. The Tully marriage was his notion, never doubt it, he—”

spoken_quote: make them privy to all our shames and secrets, a part of every council. And before too long, the ruler has become the ruled
paraphrase: Dustin says lords make maesters privy to all their shames and secrets, a part of every council, and before too long the ruler has become the ruled.
verbatim: “They heal, yes. I never said they were not subtle. They tend to us when we are sick and injured, or distraught over the illness of a parent or a child. Whenever we are weakest and most vulnerable, there they are. Sometimes they heal us, and we are duly grateful. When they fail, they console us in our grief, and we are grateful for that as well. Out of gratitude we give them a place beneath our roof and make them privy to all our shames and secrets, a part of every council. And before too long, the ruler has become the ruled.

paraphrase: Maesters write the history books, educate young lordlings, count the votes at councils, and whisper into the ears of the most powerful people in Westeros, giving them broad means to influence politics if they chose to.

Dustin claims the maesters were closely involved in a plot against the Targaryens before Robert's Rebellion, with the Starks, Tullys, and maybe the Arryns and Baratheons plotting rebellion long before the war began, led in part by a maester named Walys, said to be the son of an archmaester of the Citadel and a 'Hightower girl' -- House Hightower being one of the biggest supporters of the maesters.

Marwyn the Mage is an unconventional archmaester who keeps company with whores and hedge wizards, sacrifices to 'queer gods,' spent years in the east studying with warlocks and shadowbinders and searching for lost books, taught Mirri Maz Duur to heal, and wears a Valyrian steel mask marking him an expert on magic -- lending his claims some weight.

spoken_quote: sorcery or prophecy or glass candles
paraphrase: At the end of Feast, Marwyn tells Sam that the maesters are conspiring to build a world without sorcery, prophecy, glass candles, or dragons.
verbatim: “If I tell you, they may need to kill you too.” Marywn smiled a ghastly smile, the juice of the sourleaf running red between his teeth. “Who do you think killed all the dragons the last time around? Gallant dragonslayers armed with swords?” He spat. “The world the Citadel is building has no place in it for sorcery or prophecy or glass candles, much less for dragons. Ask yourself why Aemon Targaryen was allowed to waste his life upon the Wall, when by rights he should have been raised to archmaester. His blood was why. He could not be trusted. No more than I can.”

spoken_quote: killed all the dragons the last time around
paraphrase: Marwyn implies that the maesters killed all the dragons the last time around.
verbatim: “If I tell you, they may need to kill you too.” Marywn smiled a ghastly smile, the juice of the sourleaf running red between his teeth. “Who do you think killed all the dragons the last time around? Gallant dragonslayers armed with swords?” He spat. “The world the Citadel is building has no place in it for sorcery or prophecy or glass candles, much less for dragons. Ask yourself why Aemon Targaryen was allowed to waste his life upon the Wall, when by rights he should have been raised to archmaester. His blood was why. He could not be trusted. No more than I can.”

paraphrase: Marwyn implies the maesters didn't trust Maester Aemon because he was a Targaryen.
Marwyn implies the maesters are willing to kill to further their goals.

As the historians of Westeros, the maesters would be painfully aware of the damage done to the realm by the Targaryens and their dragons through war, misrule, and madness; several Grand Maesters were also killed by Targaryen kings, giving the maesters plausible motive to oppose the dynasty.
Maesters don't trust magic because it's wild, dangerous, and hard to control or understand, which is a real challenge to the maesters' power as scholars and advisors.
When Aemon talks about serving on the Wall instead of becoming an archmaester, it sounds like it was his own choice, not an exile.
The official World of Ice and Fire app says Aemon went to the Wall so he couldn't be used politically against his brother Egg, the king -- not because the Citadel distrusted him as a Targaryen.
At least one Targaryen did serve as an archmaester at the Citadel, though admittedly a long time ago, undercutting the idea the Citadel systematically distrusts Targaryens.

The 'last time around' Marwyn refers to was about 170 years ago, when the Targaryens had 21 dragons; most were killed during the Dance of the Dragons civil war -- 12 killed in battle, 5 killed by a rioting mob of commoners, 2 flew off, and 1 vanished, leaving one young dragon (Morning) alive under Targaryen control -- i.e. the dragons died in a war, not by direct maester action.
It has been suggested the last dragon hatchlings, which were stunted and small, might have been poisoned.

The maesters' official explanation for the dragons no longer growing large is that the Targaryens kept them in the King's Landing Dragonpit enclosure -- but Daenerys's dragons still seem to grow fine while kept chained up, casting doubt on that explanation.

In the Princess and the Queen and Rogue Prince novellas and The World of Ice and Fire, it doesn't look like the maesters were involved in starting the Dance of the Dragons -- Grand Maester Orwyle and Grand Maester Runciter both try to prevent the war from starting.
Maester Norren helps a dragon and its rider flee the Dance of the Dragons war.
If you want to get paranoid, these in-universe accounts of the Dance are written by maesters, who may have covered up their own involvement in the war.
A septon and a court jester also contributed to these accounts of the Dance, making a maester cover-up seem less likely.

The Dance of the Dragons was in large part caused by House Hightower -- Queen Alicent Hightower wanted her son Aegon to inherit over King Viserys's chosen heir Rhaenyra -- and House Hightower is the same house Lady Dustin connects to the maester (Walys) who helped cause Robert's Rebellion, and the same house known to closely support the maesters.
It doesn't seem likely the Hightowers were conspiring with the maesters against the Targaryens, because the Hightowers have actually been very loyal to the Targaryens.
It might be that the maesters manipulated the Hightowers into starting the Dance of the Dragons, in the same way the maesters apparently manipulated the Starks into rebelling against the Targaryens.

spoken_quote: Magic had died in the west when the Doom fell on Valyria
paraphrase: Daenerys says magic had died in the west when the Doom fell on Valyria; since the Doom happened centuries ago and far away, the maesters probably weren't involved in magic's original decline (though the Faceless Men might have been).
verbatim: Viserys had told her that the last Targaryen dragons had died no more than a century and a half ago, during the reign of Aegon III, who was called the Dragonbane. That did not seem so long ago to Dany. “Everywhere?” she said, disappointed. “Even in the east?” Magic had died in the west when the Doom fell on Valyria and the Lands of the Long Summer, and neither spell-forged steel nor stormsingers nor dragons could hold it back, but Dany had always heard that the east was different. It was said that manticores prowled the islands of the Jade Sea, that basilisks infested the jungles of Yi Ti, that spellsingers, warlocks, and aeromancers practiced their arts openly in Asshai, while shadowbinders and bloodmages worked terrible sorceries in the black of night. Why shouldn’t there be dragons too?

paraphrase: Maester Luwin says magic does not work.
verbatim: “All those who study the higher mysteries try their own hand at spells, soon or late. I yielded to the temptation too, I must confess it. Well, I was a boy, and what boy does not secretly wish to find hidden powers in himself? I got no more for my efforts than a thousand boys before me, and a thousand since. Sad to say, magic does not work.”

spoken_quote: Perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer
paraphrase: Maester Luwin says perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer -- a view shared by most other maesters.
verbatim: “Perhaps magic was once a mighty force in the world, but no longer. What little remains is no more than the wisp of smoke that lingers in the air after a great fire has burned out, and even that is fading. Valyria was the last ember, and Valyria is gone. The dragons are no more, the giants are dead, the children of the forest forgotten with all their lore.

paraphrase: Part of a maester's Citadel training involves spending a night in a darkened room with an obsidian/dragonglass 'glass candle'; lighting it requires magic, and the whole point of the exercise is to show maesters that magic doesn't work -- raising the question of what sense a conspiracy against magic makes if maesters believe there is no magic anymore.

Quaithe and Marwyn have both realized that glass candles are burning again -- magic is returning, with new dragons born, the Others returned, Melisandre's magic growing stronger, and resurrections happening.

spoken_quote: Old powers waken. Shadows stir. An age of wonder and terror will soon be upon us, an age for gods and heroes
paraphrase: Leo Tyrell says old powers waken, shadows stir, and an age of wonder and terror -- an age for gods and heroes -- will soon be upon us, framed as something the maesters would not want to happen.
verbatim: “Dragons and darker things,” said Leo. “The grey sheep have closed their eyes, but the mastiff sees the truth. Old powers waken. Shadows stir. An age of wonder and terror will soon be upon us, an age for gods and heroes.” He stretched, smiling his lazy smile. “That’s worth a round, I’d say.”

paraphrase: The maesters aren't secretive about magic -- they openly discuss it in their books and have an official Conclave seat for an archmaester who's an expert on magic -- so it's unclear how a conspiracy against magic would even work.
In Book 1, when the small council discusses what to do about Daenerys, Pycelle advocates assassinating her -- though most of the rest of the council does too, so it might not mean much on its own.

During Robert's Rebellion, Pycelle convinced the Mad King to open the gates of King's Landing to Tywin Lannister's army, leading to the Sack of King's Landing, the death of King Aerys, and the end of the line of Targaryen kings.

Under sharp interrogation by Tyrion and then Cersei, Pycelle breaks down weeping and reveals his true motivation was not a conspiracy against the Targaryens but a massive man-crush on Tywin Lannister -- he says he's Tywin's man, hoped Tywin would become king, and that all he did, he did for House Lannister.
verbatim: Pycelle’s breathing was rapid and shallow. “All I did, I did for House Lannister.” A sheen of sweat covered the broad dome of the old man’s brow, and wisps of white hair clung to his wrinkled skin. “Always . . . for years . . . your lord father, ask him, I was ever his true servant . . . ’twas I who bid Aerys open his gates . . .”

paraphrase: In The World of Ice and Fire, Pycelle says the gods made and shaped Tywin to rule.
Pycelle goes on about 'the man beneath the armor,' referring to Tywin.
It was loyalty to Lannister, not a conspiracy against Targaryens, that led Pycelle to bring about the Mad King's downfall.
You could claim Pycelle's doddering-old-man act is actually a master of deception hiding true involvement in a conspiracy -- he couldn't be ruled out unless you could see what he was really thinking.

Maester Cressen, who serves Stannis at Dragonstone, tries to kill the 'red witch' Melisandre in the Prologue of Clash of Kings -- which looks exactly like something someone in a conspiracy against magic would do.
The Prologue is written from Cressen's POV, showing his thoughts; a sort of ideological battle between Melisandre's magic and Cressen's denial of it is part of his motivation.
Cressen's main motivation is that he wants to protect Stannis, whom he loves and thinks of as the son he never had.
Cressen feels Melisandre is leading Stannis astray by convincing him to kill his brother and to sail to war without having allies.
Cressen's real reason for trying to kill Melisandre is fatherly love for Stannis, not a maester conspiracy against magic.
Marwyn's claims are probably exaggerated -- he might have an ulterior motive for scaring Sam off from telling the Citadel about Daenerys.

Many characters are converging on the Citadel: Sam Tarly has been sent to study and earn his maester's chain, Jaqen H'ghar has come in disguise and acquired a key said to unlock every door in the Citadel, Sarella Martell (Oberyn's bastard daughter) has come in disguise as the acolyte Alleras, and Euron Greyjoy's ironborn have started raiding around Oldtown -- suggesting more about the maesters will be revealed.
